sous_titres.text: I can't see the trenches can be any worse than this.
Nice of them to give us a bird.
I'll hang back when you're giving them the sad news.
They're taking on down at The Choral.
Class barrier's coming down. About time.
fat chance of a revolution here.
and we'll be fodder for the front.
Watch where you're going! Bloody hooligans.
-Thank you for your kindness. -No! Thank you.
Mind you, 'tis a special rate for recruits.
I'd be coining it if they fetched them home.
Aren't you in the choir at St. Wilfred's?
Mr. Trickett and me are both in The Choral.
Aye. I can dance too, if necessary.
-Not in the Matthew Passion. -Don't be shy. We won't eat you.
and, anyway, they reckon to be quite choosy.
Although, now there's no night school, I'm going to be at a loose end.
-What happened to night school? -No teacher. Joined up.
What for? You're no further on when you're done.
You've still got the status quo.
Excuse me, is this where they're doing the auditions?
-Ramsden Choral Society? -I believe it is, yes.
In fact, we were just going in ourselves, weren't we?
Take heart of grace, thy steps retrace
Not today, thank you. We're busy.
{\an8}I've come for the audition. Mary Lockwood.
They always are, the Salvation Army.
Can help thee find true peace of mind...
We're contraltos. Are you a contralto?
I'm... I just like to sing.
Well, you belong over there. We're not auditioning.
Can help thee find true peace of mind
This is Clyde's girlfriend. Missing in action.
He was the best tenor we ever had.
"Was"? He's only missing. It's not been confirmed.
I always hold in having it if you fancy it
And suppose it makes you fat? I don't worry over that
'Cause a little of what you fancy
there's one or two new faces in the hall today.
I'm Alderman Duxbury. And you all know me from the mill.
But this isn't the mill, it's The Choral.
The person you've got to watch out for isn't me.
It's the chorus master, Mr. Pollard.
Keep on the right side of him and you won't go far wrong.
The point is, should you be lucky enough to be selected,
you will be expected to comport yourselves accordingly.
I hope I make myself plain.
Don't worry, Mr. Duxbury, I've got a sacred side.
I mean, you lad, you're black bright.
I feel it's my patriotic duty.
Why don't you wait for your call-up papers?
Well, what about these today? Do we take them or not?
-Uh, some of the lasses are good. -It's not lasses we want.
Uh, the lads are all right.
Good strong voices. They just want a little encouragement.
I hope he puts the wind up the Germans more than he ever did the sopranos.
Don't worry about me, Mr. Duxbury. Too smart to get shot.
Oh, God, that's not to say...
Off you go and good luck.
We'll find someone to keep us in shape while you're away.
No grip. The Choral needs grip.
I'm going to say a name.
But I don't want to be shot down.
No one, yet. But when I open my mouth...
It's the committee, love. I told you.
We're looking for a new chorus master.
Pollard's gone and joined up, the great barmpot.
he wouldn't even consider the likes of us.
He's been living and working in Germany. By choice.
He had musical opportunities. You can understand it.
He had musical opportunities here. But he preferred Germany
That's why Leeds got rid of him.
We can't, Joe. Not if his sympathies are German.
Besides, there was talk of other things.
Let's just say, I'd prefer a family man.
You'd prefer the Archbishop of Canterbury.
He'd never come anyway. He'll be conducting somewhere
they don't care about his peculiarities.
He's playing at the Queens Hotel.
Well, I like it here. It, um...
We all have livings to earn.
But, meanwhile, there's music to be made.
And you have to realize that if we were to offer you this post...
And that disqualifies me from conducting a choir?
Dr. Guthrie, I'm offering you an opportunity.
The Matthew Passion at Ramsden Town Hall.
Only we need peace of mind.
What did you do it for, and where do you stand?
With regards to the St. Matthew Passion?
"A man should hear a little music, read a little poetry,
"and see a fine picture every day of his life,
"in order that worldly cares may not obliterate
"the sense of the beautiful which God has implanted
Oh, that is nice. Who said it?
For God's sake, man, lower your voice.
Wouldn't you stay in a country of cultured, civilized people?
People who put music and beauty, art,
at the center of everything they do?
The English think music is just social life
carried on by other means. I don't.
You see, it was musically motivated.
I would need to have control.
Well, I'm the chairman, that can't change.
I find I'm most often called upon to sing the tenor solos.
Well, there's no point disrupting the choir
And I should like to bring my own pianist.
Unpaid, mind. We'd be doing him a favor.
We're doing you both a favor, let's face it.
Not half as much a favor as you're doing us.
-Thank you for your kindness. -Where did they say he's from?
Only he went and lived in Germany.
{\an8}I'm afraid this is the end. I shall be obliged to leave.
Aye, and found nothing amiss, so here he is.
If they found nothing amiss, young man,
then explain his behavior in the public library.
He goes in to read the papers. All of them.
Every morning. And when he's done,
and Miss Pendle goes in to smooth the sheets,
they're always open at the same page.
"My Father, if it's possible, allow this cup to pass from me."
Although, no doubt, you sing it in English.
He'll be accompanying you for today's auditions. Thank you.
I thought we're done with the auditions?
Not for me, you haven't. And, um...
Well, that was a... a very stimulating afternoon.
I'm sure I speak for the entire society when I...
The fact is the Town Hall is booked and the word is out.
The St. Matthew Passion will be performed in a few short weeks.
They were, but their Jesus went and joined up.
It's a bit of an old warhorse, but it's a piece we know.
Some of us can do it with our eyes closed.
I'd like to hear the chorale.
And eyes open, if you please.
Don't stop. Sing on, sing on!
The way you were singing, it was probably a critic.
Even sung in English, Bach is out?
There's a danger to life and limb.
Well, he ended up English, but he started off German.
Well, I suppose it depends how much of a pedant
There's Brahms, of course, who I've met.
But he, alas, is also... a Hun.
We could be in the pub now,
instead of hanging about here arguing the toss.
He's a bit posh, is that pianist.
Ask Lofty, news is the one thing you don't want.
She thinks if you love someone, you can just tell.
I had that privilege. Although it was in Germany,
so you might not think it a suitable topic for conversation.
"Free but lonely." It was something he wrote.
I wish we were doing his Requiem.
And then they'd really have something to throw bricks about.
There must be someone they don't object to.
that it might be me that folk object to?
-Just as much as the music. -That's as maybe.
For God's sake, man, rein in the bloody German.
He started off as an organist, same as me.
Have you met him and all?
In Germany, he's a god. He's up there with Wagner.
He wrote an oratorio a few years back.
It premiered in Birmingham Town Hall.
Predictably, the audience hated it. If it hadn't been religious,
it would have been booed off the stage.
-I loved it. -Why did they hate it?
Of course, Gerontius is a tenor.
In terms of permission, or skill?
Meanwhile, we have to face facts.
It needs a huge chorus and male voices are at a premium,
Do you want a choir, Mr. Duxbury, or do you want a Sunday School?
Have any of you got any pals, any young men who can sing?
Does it matter where? I've got one sings in a pub.
He can sing in a brothel, as far as I'm concerned.
Singing is a kind of exposure
and if I'm going to be letting my hair down,
I don't want to be next to my window cleaner's wife.
Now, surely you ladies know one or two gentlemen
I'm used to pubs. I just wave my tin.
It's a long, long way to Tipperary
I think he'll... he'll do. And these three too.
-Mitch, this is Dr. Guthrie. -Doctor? Why, who's poorly?
-From The Choral. -Ah, well, they won't want me.
-I live down Theaker Lane. -I don't know where people live.
A choral society shouldn't mirror the social order.
-Oh, hello. Are you in it? -I might be.
I like the way you rattle your tin.
Makes me want to put a penny in your slot.
Yes. And me to give you a crack across the face.
Well, I'll have to join the choir then, won't I, Ellis?
Haven't you done it before? The Dream of Gerontius?
-Why did you not say? -Because it was in Nuremberg.
It seems like everything good that happened to you,
Will he give his permission, do you think, Elgar?
He will if I write to him.
The man may be our greatest living composer
but he's a fellow musician, however grand.
Artists are like that, Robert, they notice each other.
Well, uh, not all of them.
They're not in a state to sing, most of them.
What have they got to sing about? They're convalescent.
They don't want to waste their energy on singing.
This gentleman wants to know if any of you can sing?
Three little maids from school are we
Pert as a schoolgirl well can be
Filled to the brim with girlish glee
Nobody's safe for we care for none
Life is a joke that's just begun
Morning, Podge. This gentleman wants to know if you can sing.
We can work on the other.
Proclaim the word, "praise." The note will be there.
Uh, a little faster on figure four.
Mind you, Mam says there'll be deferment.
What for? Singing in the choir?
Them, in London. They decide it. Old, and well off.
When we go makes no difference to them.
I'm patriotic, but in me own good time.
Carted off to France. I haven't lived.
That's what ought to get you deferred.
Hey, don't look now, but there's Mrs. Bishop.
Why won't the other women talk to her at The Choral?
You might be more familiar with the sound of me, Mrs. Bishop,
My mate here sits next to me.
Perhaps we'll meet in a less formal setting soon.
Don't count on it, you cheeky sod.
Having to go at 18. Not having done it.
I can't be the only one. No fuck, no front.
That ought to be the decider.
Well, I've done it, and it doesn't make it any more acceptable.
Though I think once you're over there, they cater for all that.
But it oughtn't to be just intercourse.
-I've never tasted champagne. -I have. It's rubbish.
I've not been in a proper motor car.
I've never even seen the sea.
-You've been to Morecambe. -It wasn't in that day.
Have you ever been in love, Lofty?
I don't wanna throw it away on anybody.
Well, you can always bestow it on Mrs. Bishop.
Though she charges. Even for bestowing.
Where've you lads been? Come on!
Mary'd have to take her bonnet off.
She gets enough of that from Mitch.
He can swim. Look at him.
Clyde and me used to come down here
Not even a cossie, he was that bonny.
-I don't wanna know! -You do, I can tell.
And you don't wanna be left like me.
The others never leave you alone.
Ellis is right enough, but it's only 'cause I'm available.
We've got the Queens Hotel to look forward to.
Guthrie is treating all the new recruits to their teas.
Not recruits to the army, stupid. Recruits to the choir.
You'd never think there was a war on.
Oh, is it, uh, a funeral?
With a civilian funeral, of course it is.
And it's the first I've had in a fortnight. It's tragic.
Everybody's bereaved and I'm going bankrupt.
This is what we're fighting for.
Don't get overexcited, only your friend
has divested herself of her helmet.
What's she dancing with Bella for?
There's plenty of men. She could be dancing with me.
I'd like to dance with Ellis, only Flo could make remarks.
I'll ask her out for ya.
I almost wish the telegram would come.
Then everyone would know that Clyde is dead
Why aren't you at the front? Why aren't you fighting?
Because I'm singing. We all are.
And we're none of us 18 yet, and when we are we'll go.
you can take your flaming feather
and tickle your fanny with it because nobody else is going to.
Yes, will you tell us a bit more about it?
Some of us are still in the dark.
As the name suggests, Gerontius, Mr. Duxbury,
is an old man who, in the first part of the oratorio,
It's quite short and ends with his death.
In the second part, there is some delay
while the destiny of Gerontius' soul is decided.
You see? You always have to queue.
In this case, it's because the forces of good and evil
with the music given to the demons very dramatic,
while the Angel, and that will be Mary...
Which she could be, after all.
The salvation of Gerontius is what she wants.
To cut it short, the Angel wins,
and the oratorio ends with her conveying his soul to Purgatory
with a promise to lead him from there into the presence of God.
-Not much of a story, is it? -You don't have to believe it.
Well, the words, of course, are very beautiful. John Henry Newman.
"Be brave and patient on my bed of sorrow.
"Swiftly shall pass thy night of trial here.
"And I will come and wake thee on the morrow."
What difference does it make? He could be a Mohammedan.
Mr. Duxbury, you're not singing, you are crooning.
And, contraltos, have you anything against a B flat?
Well, you never go anywhere near it.
Mr. Duxbury, could we look at figure 40, please?
Here. You need to support your voice a little bit more.
I think we have to acknowledge this crescendo here...
If she's ever daft enough to go out with you,
she'd still have God as her fancy man.
Well, it's all right for you.
You've latched yourself on very nicely.
Worst of it is, I've only just found out her second name.
I'm never gonna get anywhere with an Elspeth.
-Stanley, you're late. -Never mind that, Dr. Guthrie.
839 Fritzes dead and floating in the North bloody Sea!
I forgot. This is for you, sir.
If only you sang Elgar with the confidence
I think that was Elgar's intention, Mr. Duxbury.
Are you all right, Mr. Duxbury?
I just wish I had a better voice. I enjoy it so much.
No, it's... it's a nice voice.
I'm sure people think I'm only singing Gerontius
because I put my hand in my pocket.
Without the mill, there'd be no choir.
Not with me always putting me spoke in.
I pay for the flaming choir, it entitles me to some say.
You mustn't say I said this.
I just wish he wasn't so...
What's Elgar like? Do you know anything?
He's coming to Manchester the day of the concert.
They're giving him an honorary degree.
Well, if we wrote to him, he might come over for the performance.
-I was going to ask Dr. Guthrie. -I wouldn't.
He won't be grateful. He never is.
I got my papers this morning.
I wish you liked girls, like the other lads do.
-What's that supposed to mean? -Mind you, if you did...
lasses probably wouldn't like you so much.
He joined up in the first wave. Practically first in the queue.
At least you've got more sense.
He had a good voice. Unspoiled.
And instead we're stuck with Alderman Duxbury.
We... we must get you deferred.
I'm going to register as a conscientious objector.
-Well, you'll go to prison. -I thought you'd approve.
-I'm thinking of the choir. -Of course you are.
Well, Duxbury's on the tribunal. He can get you off.
The vicars want it. The women want it.
The idiots getting killed, they want it. Who do you turn to?
-I'd like to help, if I can. -You could stay out of prison.
replace one friend with another, but...
This oratorio about an old man.
It's the young men who are dying.
This house can't become a mausoleum, Margaret.
We can't live in total silence forever.
You're free to live entirely as you please, Bernard.
Oh, he's here, he's here! Rick! All right?
Look after yourself, all right, lad, yeah?
Head down, yeah? You'll be fine.
-Eh, behave yourself over there. -Next lot won't be so cheerful.
I've seen fellas dropping all around me,
never seen anyone going down like that.
What did you do? Were you wounded?
Well, I didn't fall off me bike.
-Same dirty girl, I hope. -Don't do that.
Why not? You used to like it.
Till death fetches me in the proper way.
I could even come back to The Choral.
Why not? It'd give me something to do.
I'm not conducting the bloody thing.
Get back to the way things were.
Besides, I know the Matthew Passion like the back of my hand.
You'd have to speak to Guthrie.
Bit sarky. Folk are terrified of him.
I'm just back from the front, Bella, I think I'll manage.
I just called in to see if things had changed.
and, of course, I know Mr. Horner
from The Choral Society where he is our much-valued pianist.
Uh, Dr. Guthrie has come along just as an observer.
Uh... I prefer to think of this as a... a friendly conversation.
This young man is objecting to doing military service.
I have your statement. What puzzles me about you people
is that you always think you're privy to the mind of God.
"War is not part of God's plan."
Well, I'm a canon of Ripon Cathedral,
so I'm more familiar with the mind of God than you are,
and war does come into God's plan and always has.
You've only to read the Old Testament, it's war, war, war.
I wasn't aware they were alternatives.
Mr. Horner is a talented pianist.
That doesn't stop him kicking a ball.
We're talking about firing a gun.
I'm finding it hard to have any sympathy with you.
But the only creatures who get the benefit are the pheasants.
Not a vegetarian, by any chance?
I can't understand how you have any moral compass at all.
Let alone any contact with sacred music.
I don't rule out serving in another capacity.
will be relieved to know that.
I thought I could be a stretcher bearer.
You don't seem to be aware
that you belong to a privileged generation.
The first, in our island's history,
to be granted the honor of being conscripted.
Knowing Mr. Horner, I think I would advise the Tribunal
that you would benefit from a brief period of deferment.
Say, three months, to think it over.
Then I will put it to the chorus master.
Is Mr. Horner essential to the society's next concert?
You should be grateful to be needed.
There's lads going off every day. Nobody needs them.
Except the country. I'd be happy to go.
You have to learn to play the game.
I don't want any special treatment. I'm happy to be a conchy.
You, you're no different from the bloody generals.
-That's what's wrong with the world. -You're young, that's what's wrong.
You sound like Duxbury. Or Field Marshal Haig.
In less than a month's time, we hope this hall will be full.
And Alderman Duxbury has yet to give us even an approximation
Well, I used to love The Choral, when it was Bella and me.
I can't see her every day, though. Not without...
Well, you've been dealt some rough cards.
I'm going to say something now and it may seem insensitive,
Ironically, you've all the ingredients here for genius.
Not to mention the things you've seen.
-I'd rather be happy. -So would every other bugger.
But there are people who, funnily enough,
would give their right arm to be able to do what you can do.
And I don't know whether it's cruel or kind,
but life has offered you a consolation prize.
is the moment you're best equipped to use it.
How many more times? You're not having Fridays.
Just this once. Guthrie wants to see me.
Look, neither of you will be having anything
What's it look like, you hanging about on me doorstep?
He's too young. His voice isn't heavy enough.
I can play older, he can't.
The lad has a miraculous voice, Bernard.
Alderman. I won't have you appealing to my better nature.
Like you, I don't have one.
It's this flaming war. It's ruined everything.
We used to have such a nice going-on.
It's my own fault for appointing you in the first place.
If I cared less about the music, I could put me foot down.
We're still short of a devil.
-Is there a solo devil? -The way we're doing it, yes.
And it has you written all over it.
I wish I didn't like singing.
Ladies and gentlemen, we have a new Gerontius.
But in the absence of a full symphony orchestra,
and a chorus of a hundred,
we must tailor Elgar's Dream to our present circumstances.
To begin with, our Gerontius isn't old.
He is a young man. A soldier.
And, perhaps, the Angel who delivers him from Purgatory
And heaven, if not quite the presence of God,
is home, and England, and peace.
-And is that the orchestra? -We may be a chamber ensemble,
but I don't think there'll be any complaints.
It'll be quite short, and not a full-blown oratorio
by any manner of means. But it will be as heartfelt
as that original one in Birmingham Town Hall.
Less holy, maybe, but more topical.
-From the nethermost fire -From all that is evil
-From the power of the devil -Thy servant deliver
That's good! I am proud of you. Elgar would be proud of you.
Two weeks to go, and we're finally getting somewhere.
I'm still not happy about Purgatory.
I... I appreciate that the author is... is Roman Catholic.
But the Church of England is very clear.
There is no such place as Purgatory.
Listen, I could take you there tomorrow.
Purgatory starts ten yards from the front line
and it's called No Man's Land.
Every time we mount another damn fool attack,
we cross it, if we're lucky.
They get driven back by their officers,
and get shot if they don't.
We're to report to the recruiting office
Oh, take no notice of me, lads. I was just...
It's not that bad if you keep your head down.
-I knew he was exaggerating. -Were you a good soldier?
-Yes, I didn't get killed. -Just lost an arm.
When you're over there, see if you can find it.
If there's time, I'd like to see a bit of the countryside.
What, if there's an half day, you mean?
-When we get the vote. -War, war, war.
You lot talk about nothing else.
Mary, Mary. Shall I... see you home?
I'm not getting anywhere, am I?
I don't know what you see in her.
She won't even let you kiss her.
That's what I see in her.
-Shit, it's her mother. -You two, off you go.
Are you the one that's been courting our Mary?
I don't know what you're talking about.
You know full well. Answer me, yes or no.
We hadn't done anything. I mean, kissed even!
That doesn't surprise me. Takes after her grandma.
Nobody ever laid a finger on her, either.
I don't mind Jesus, but not while we're having our tea.
Sounds as if I'd have been better off with you.
I never fancied him as a son-in-law.
-Open your towel. -He was in our class at school.
Had to get something between his teeth.
Is this right? Are you epileptic?
Not so as to stop me shooting Fritz.
Why does he look at your willy?
Come on, lad. Off you go.
Don't worry, love. They'll be okay.
Well, you came back, didn't you?
And I don't mind about Ellis.
He's right enough, if you're happy.
I didn't think it'd make that much difference,
but it's surprising how much you need both of 'em.
-I don't want to ask. -Nay, go on.
If I can help, I will.
I can't do it with the left hand, it gets tired.
-You never minded before. -I have to think of Ellis.
Just the one. I'll train myself to do it with the other.
I can, if I put my mind to it.
He must be a decent chap.
Decent, I don't know. Magnificent, yes.
Generous enough to allow us to tinker with his stuff.
-You haven't told him, have you? -He gave his permission.
I mean all this new carry-on.
No orchestra, the cutting, the rearrangement.
We are a fiddling little minor provincial choral society.
They're giving him an honorary degree.
-Yes, I saw. -The day of the concert.
We could ask him to come.
No, certainly not. He's a great man, he's got better things to do.
He's an old man. Maybe he hasn't.
Oh, be told, the pair of you.
Save your bright ideas for adapting the score.
Why can't we put it into costume...
Clyde dressed as a soldier, Mary as a nurse.
This is an oratorio. Dressing up is opera.
Well, we've broken some rules, so why can't we break others?
No, you're right. We... We are nobody.
Our Gerontius is a one-armed youth.
The orchestra is the trio from the Queens Hotel.
Opera, oratorio, who's going to care?
I was somebody once, but... that was in Germany.
Never known him accept a suggestion from anyone else, ever.
-He does like me, I suppose. -I do, too.
What's he gonna think of all of this?
Well, I've done it now. Sorry.
I want to go. I shouldn't have said.
-Are these for us? -For the last rehearsal.
I'm surprised you're still making these.
I still don't understand why we're killing folks
who like the same fancies as we do.
I mean, we've got common ground.
How much do they cost, this model?
I'm going to take this in four, and slightly slower,
and keep an eye on this...
Uh, may I assist you, sir?
I thought that was the point of it?
They don't like me singing on street corners.
I don't mind where I sing.
"Make a joyful noise unto the Lord."
-Exactly. -You don't want a cup of tea?
No, I want Dr. Guthrie and I don't have a lot of time.
Forgive my getup. I've been in Manchester.
Is it China where every profession has its own uniform?
This is mine, my musical degree.
In the circumstances, I feel it's quite discreet.
It's romantic, adventurous, and poorly paid.
But for once, this afternoon, I'm allowed to advertise.
I'm not just a jumped-up shop assistant.
behind the counter in my father's shop.
But what's happened to the peerage, hmm?
Still, I'm a humble man, and this is what one appreciates,
to have one's work performed by amateurs.
-It's an honor, sir. -In the back of beyond.
But, don't worry, I shall not be attending the performance
and I don't expect too much.
Nothing can be worse than The Dream's first outing
when I ran from Birmingham Town Hall with my hands over my ears.
Never mind composing, he certainly fucking talks.
I am touched that you should want to put it on.
Few societies do. Amateurs not at all.
All they want is Land of Hope and Glory.
If he wants a preview, we're in the shit.
I don't have a lot of time, but is there any chance of
you giving me a hint of what you've been doing?
I wish we could, but this evening is our performance.
-I mean, there is much to do. -No matter.
And, besides, the choir has been rehearsing all morning.
-I've sent most of them home. -Not even the young lady?
No, well, she's, um, not prepared.
And if she sings as delightfully as she looks,
I know you must be weary of your fans, Sir Edward,
but might I trouble you to sign my score?
Forget the dressing gown, he just looks like anybody else.
My late father-in-law springs to mind.
I'm actually, uh, one of the principals.
In fact, I was singing Gerontius,
until we brought it up to date.
Well, it's Cardinal Newman's poem, that's why it's religious.
But he... No, no, no, he's too young.
You'd be surprised, Sir Edward, how poignant a young man sounds.
I don't care if he was wounded at Agincourt.
His voice won't be heavy enough.
What else have you meddled with? Let me see the score.
This isn't a score, it's a scrapbook.
What have you done with the orchestration?
We are the orchestra, Sir Edward, our trio.
